name: httpx
summary: A next-generation HTTP client — sync and async, HTTP/2 ready

story.problem: requests is synchronous — it blocks your thread while waiting for the server. In an async app (FastAPI, asyncio), blocking calls stall the entire event loop. httpx offers the same clean API but with full async/await support and HTTP/2.
story.mental_model: httpx is requests with two modes: drop-in synchronous (same API you already know) and an async client you use with `async with`. The same Response object, the same method names, the same .json() — but now it won't block your event loop.
story.when_to_use: Async Python apps (FastAPI, Starlette, asyncio scripts) that make outbound HTTP calls. Also a good default replacement for requests in new projects — it has a stricter, more modern design.
story.when_not_to_use: If your codebase is already fully synchronous and uses requests everywhere, the migration cost may not be worth it. For extremely high concurrency (thousands of simultaneous connections), consider aiohttp which has lower overhead.

alternatives.name: requests
alternatives.reason: Synchronous only, but simpler and has a larger ecosystem of adapters/plugins

alternatives.name: aiohttp
alternatives.reason: Async-first, higher performance at scale, but more complex API

alternatives.name: urllib3
alternatives.reason: Lower-level; what requests and httpx are both built on

nodes.label: httpx
nodes.summary: Top-level module. Exports both sync and async clients.

nodes.label: Client
nodes.summary: Synchronous HTTP client. Use as a context manager for connection pooling.

nodes.label: AsyncClient
nodes.summary: Async HTTP client. Use with `async with` in async functions.

nodes.label: get() / post() / put() / delete()
nodes.summary: Top-level convenience functions for one-off requests (no session reuse).

nodes.label: Response
nodes.summary: Identical API to requests.Response. Status, headers, .json(), .text, .raise_for_status().

nodes.label: Timeout
nodes.summary: Fine-grained timeout control: connect, read, write, and pool timeouts separately.

nodes.label: Auth (base class)
nodes.summary: Subclass to implement custom auth flows (OAuth, HMAC signing, etc.).

nodes.label: HTTPStatusError
nodes.summary: Raised by response.raise_for_status() on 4xx/5xx. Carries .request and .response.

nodes.label: TimeoutException
nodes.summary: Raised when the request exceeds any configured timeout.

steps.label: One-off request
steps.code:
import httpx

r = httpx.get('https://httpbin.org/get')
print(r.status_code)
print(r.json())

steps.label: With session (recommended)
steps.code:
with httpx.Client() as client:
    r = client.get('https://api.example.com/users')
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()
steps.explanation: Using a Client reuses the connection pool across multiple requests — faster and fewer sockets.

tasks.title: Async GET in an async function

steps.label: Async function
steps.code:
async def fetch_user(user_id: int):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        r = await client.get(
            f'https://api.example.com/users/{user_id}'
        )
        r.raise_for_status()
        return r.json()

steps.label: Run
steps.explanation: In FastAPI, you'd just call await client.get(...) directly — no asyncio.run() needed.

steps.label: Define fetch
steps.code:
async def fetch_all(urls: list[str]):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        tasks = [client.get(url) for url in urls]
        responses = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    return [r.json() for r in responses]

steps.label: Run
steps.explanation: All three requests fire simultaneously. Total time ≈ slowest single request, not the sum.

steps.label: Custom timeout
steps.code:
timeout = httpx.Timeout(
    5.0,        # default for all
    connect=2.0 # stricter connect timeout
)

steps.label: Apply to client
